story:
Vanilla extract on her broken arm did nothing. Three girls online swore it would, and Jaylee’s sister claimed it cured her pink eye so thoroughly her eyes stopped watering, she could wear her glasses again, and she even took up sharpshooting. Maybe Jaylee didn’t believe hard enough. Hunter said belief was the point. Cinnamon could heal dementia if you trusted it to; same with religion, same with everything.

They were on their fourth break of seven during a seventy-nine-hour shift, wandering the arts-and-crafts store across from the Department. Hunter’s phone buzzed—her boyfriend, the one fired from the aquarium for an affair with a mermaid. Forgiveness turned to polyamory, which turned to a mermaid in their bathtub and a chase for overtime to fund a move to a place with a jacuzzi. Sea-salt bonsai candle in hand, Hunter asked if Jaylee still wanted the promotion. It paid more, even came with corneal insurance, but also more responsibility. Hunter needed to be home; otherwise Steve and Aquatica might try for a baby without her.

Jaylee wasn’t planning to apply. The money felt real, but so did the cost.

They rushed back before a lecture from Pinball. Pinball, their boss, was a colossal pinball machine—the largest on Earth—who ran the Department that ushered confused people into their unending outcomes. When new arrivals demanded to know why they’d spend forever inside a painting of a supermarket produce section, Pinball let them play him until the panic smoothed out, then shouted at the staff for being useless. He was always being used and always angry about it. No one wanted to set him off. Everyone did.

Jaylee had broken her arm in his office. At the end of a ninety-one-hour day, he offered her a free ball. Employees weren’t supposed to play him; the offer felt wrong, but “no” felt dangerous. Lights, thrashing flippers, a score that maxed out and rolled over—she kept going, close to number two on the leaderboard, when something snapped. The pain followed a second later. HR needed her to confirm it wasn’t Pinball’s fault. Then he dangled the promotion again: weekly games, bones that would harden after they broke, vision dulling and sharpening, hair falling out, and a promised high score that would reveal truths no guru could teach.

She said it sounded great, then paid out of pocket at urgent care because she didn’t have pinball-injury insurance. Later, what gnawed at her wasn’t pain; it was the time, the hands on his side rails, the proximity, the way a job promotion might be a story about touching his start button. Still, the money: an apartment instead of a pharmacy cot wedged beneath toothbrushes—not the magazine kiosk her coworker slept in, but something like Hunter’s place, quietly subsidized by Hunter’s father’s teleportation settlement.

When Pinball called her in after the break, a ball clattered inside him. His office was glass, high over a city designed like a maze: long streets feeding shorter ones until a concrete circle where food trucks gathered and gulls swarmed. People without housing—people who couldn’t afford even a shelf’s rent between toothpaste and deodorant—tried to catch seagulls to cook over trash-can fires in the alleys off the shortest streets. Jaylee felt how close she was to that edge. The strangest part was they weren’t anywhere near the water. She had never seen the ocean, but she knew it was deep and dark and could expand with heat whenever it wanted.

“Want to play?” Pinball asked.

“Tomorrow,” she said. Tomorrow her arm would feel better.
